item.title: The Strategic Logic of the AI Arms Race
item.description: AI and drones are rapidly transforming warfare, shifting military power toward countries that can combine manufacturing scale, advanced AI systems, and battlefield data. The United States and Europe, hobbled by political dysfunction and obsolete military structures, remain dangerously unprepared.

item.title: The Meaning of Limited Government—Then and Now
item.description: In 1776, Adam Smith and America’s founders envisioned an economy that allowed individuals to pursue their own material well-being, with minimal government intervention. Today, America’s Republican Party claims to be committed to restoring that laissez-faire ideal, even as they make a mockery of its animating principles.

item.title: No Reset at G7: Trump Was Trump, Modi Was Not Modi
item.description: The Modi-Trump meeting revealed the limits of India’s willingness to speak with moral clarity when confronted with American power.

item.title: Xi’s Authoritarian Blueprint for the Myanmar Junta
item.description: Xi implied to Min Aung Hlaing that brute military force is failing, and he must instead master CCP-style totalitarian controls to pacify the public.

item.title: The Iran War Damaged U.S.-India Ties
item.description: Diplomatic fractures between the two strategic partners are likely to outlast the conflict.

item.title: Floating Volcanic Rock Is Disrupting Life in Papua New Guinea
item.description: The ongoing Titan Ridge eruption has left parts of PNG’s coast covered in meters of floating volcanic rock. For a community dependent on the ocean, that's a serious problem.

item.title: AI Sovereignty Is About Options, Not Ownership
item.description: The US government’s abrupt decision to suspend foreign access to Anthropic’s Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models clarifies what “AI sovereignty” is really about. In the emerging AI economy, competitive advantage will come not from owning a single model, but from being able to evaluate, select, and orchestrate many models.

item.title: Wang Yi’s Visit and Mongolia’s Foreign Policy Balancing Act

item.title: The Promise and Peril of AGI
item.description: Artificial general intelligence could dramatically increase human prosperity and free people from countless mundane tasks. But unless its benefits and ownership are broadly shared, it could deprive billions of workers of their livelihoods and pave the way for a new form of techno-authoritarianism.

item.title: The United States Has a Kafala Problem

item.title: The Fed Has Been Honest and Stupid

item.description: The merger is the latest step in the consolidation – or rebranding – of power under President Kassym-Jomart Tokayev. The names change; the system stays the same.

item.title: The Middle East’s New Normal
item.description: The most likely outcome of the Iran war is not peace but an unsatisfying ceasefire that leaves the underlying issues unresolved. This will mean an uneasy equilibrium in which Gulf countries go their own way, US influence wanes, and the regional order remains unsettled.

item.title: Bangladesh-Turkiye Defense Cooperation Grows to Include Joint Production
item.description: Drones are the centerpiece of the bilateral cooperation. The two countries are also discussing manufacture of military hardware in Bangladesh.

item.title: No Bridge Over Healing Waters for the G7
item.description: The French G7 presidency’s focus on global imbalances is both welcome and problematic. While long-standing trade imbalances between the United States, Europe, and China are, if anything, becoming even more troublesome, the leaders’ summit in Évian-les-Bains will do nothing to address the problem.

item.title: Are Government Stakes the Key to AI Sovereignty?
item.description: At first glance, reports that the US and Chinese governments are both considering taking stakes in national AI champions would seem to suggest a convergence between the world’s two leading AI powers. But a closer look reveals stark differences in their underlying strategies.

item.title: Breaking Afghanistan’s Hydro-Political Trap
item.description: Since its neighbors rely on unregulated river flows, any unilateral Afghan attempt to develop water infrastructure is perceived as a threat, risking regional destabilization.

item.title: Matthew Campbell on the ‘Man Whole Stole the Gods’
item.description: A new book examines the life of Douglas Latchford, a British antiquities trader who was responsible for the mass looting of Cambodia's Angkorian heritage.

item.title: Chinese Authorities Arrest American Myanmar Researcher For Alleged Espionage
item.description: Min Zin, the head of a prominent Myanmar-focused think-tank, has been accused of "endangering China’s national security."

item.title: Introducing the Summer 2026 Print Issue
item.description: The end of the world as we know it.

item.title: The End of Neoliberalism
item.description: The virtues it extolled—cosmopolitanism and competition—led to its demise.

item.title: The End of the U.S.-Israel Alliance
item.description: A joint war against Iran might be its apex.

item.title: The End of Trans-Atlanticism
item.description: As an ideology, it’s over. But the relationship is not.

item.title: The End of Climate Politics
item.description: As the West debated a green energy future, Beijing was building it.

item.title: The End of the United Nations
item.description: To its most powerful member, it is worth more alive than dead.

item.title: The End of Asylum
item.description: States across the global north are shutting their doors permanently.

item.title: The End of Political Parties
item.description: They are hardly prepared for the new system.

item.title: The End of Chinese Growth
item.description: Beijing’s economic ambitions have been brought back to earth.

item.title: The End of Morality
item.description: We may come to miss the hypocrisy.

item.title: The End of the Future
item.description: Only Silicon Valley looks ahead now.

item.title: The False Promise of U.S.-China Stability
item.description: Washington will come to regret its stalemate with Beijing.

item.title: How to Beat an Autocrat
item.description: The real lessons of Orban’s defeat.

item.title: The False Promise of U.S.-China Stability
item.description: Washington will come to regret its stalemate with Beijing.

item.title: Vietnamese Police Disrupt Online Scam Operations Relocating From Cambodia
item.description: As Phnom Penh cracks down on cyber-fraud operations, Chinese criminal syndicates are hastily seeking out more accommodating jurisdictions.

item.title: What Next for Global Trade?
From the American Revolution to Universal Suffrage
item.description: Britain’s attempt to crush the American Revolution exposed a constitutional crisis at the heart of its empire. One English statesman saw in it proof that only broader suffrage could check royal power, tame corruption, and ensure that imperial rule remained compatible with liberty.

item.title: Jefferson’s Revolutionary Language
item.description: Of the delegates who assembled in Philadelphia in 1776 for the Second Continental Congress, only Thomas Jefferson possessed the skill, knowledge, and linguistic gifts to make the Declaration of Independence an immortal text. He was the self-appointed conscience of America, and sensational imagery was his stock-in-trade.

item.title: The Declaration of the Enslaved
item.description: For African-Americans, the Declaration of Independence was nothing but a “thin veil to cover the crimes” of slavery, as Frederick Douglass put it. Inspired by Haiti’s Declaration of Independence, free and enslaved Black people in the US found themselves subverting America’s founding document to achieve their own emancipation.

item.title: Free-Market Capitalism Is Destroying US Democracy
item.description: Far from two sides of the same coin, free-market capitalism and democracy are fundamentally incompatible. The only way to restore US democracy is to transform the system, so that it compensates innovators but limits their market power, while ensuring that the benefits of economic progress are more equitably shared.

item.title: Canada’s Great Recalibration
item.description: The growing fusion of economics, security, and technology has altered the nature of 21st-century interdependence, and Canada has responded by being more intentional. Rather than automatically serving as an extension of US-centered systems, it is creating more options across a wider network of relationships.

item.title: From Jefferson to Nehru
item.description: The Declaration of Independence continues to challenge countries, including the US and India, to measure themselves against the ideals they profess. But in a century marked by rising inequality, an authoritarian resurgence, and the emergence of new forms of domination, measuring up will become only more difficult.

item.title: The Roots of Democratic Decline
item.description: Notwithstanding recent setbacks for illiberal, populist, and authoritarian leaders in some countries, good-governance groups and experts on democratic decline continue to document a disturbing global trend toward “autocratization.” What remains to be seen is whether enough voters will wake up to the threat.

item.title: The Standard of Independence
item.description: Although revolutions would surely have occurred had the United States never broken from Britain in 1776, the Declaration of Independence gave later anticolonial movements inspiration and the language to shape their own aspirations. It offered a universal blueprint, even as the US itself adopted the ethos of empire.

item.title: The Declaration of Independence Turns South
item.description: Whether they were agreeing or arguing, the new countries that emerged across the Americas after the American Revolution influenced each other. Together, they helped shape the global development of modern democracy and the social and political movements that have shaped it ever since.

item.title: Capitalism’s Creature
item.description: The American Dream, with its basis in Thomas Jefferson’s immortal words, was one of the most powerfully generative ideas ever conceived. But the economy and impersonal systems it helped to bring into being are now shaping society and its individual members, rather than the other way around.

item.title: What the Declaration Did and Did Not Say
item.description: The American founders’ understanding of political community was ethnically and racially restrictive in ways that Americans have since repudiated. But the Declaration of Independence’s underlying logic and treatment of rights and what it takes to secure them remains as cogent as it was 250 years ago.

item.title: Independence for Me, But Not for Thee
item.description: The Declaration of Independence spoke of all states as being "separate and equal," reflecting the founders' deference to the norms of international law and practice. Yet 250 years later, Donald Trump's administration has inverted this posture, much to the detriment of America's standing in the world.

item.title: China’s AI Agenda
item.description: While American and international news coverage of AI tends to focus on a handful of Silicon Valley labs, China's more pragmatic approach to leveraging the technology could ultimately be the bigger story. What the Chinese leadership sees in the technology is nothing less than a master key for its economic ambitions.

item.title: After the Liberal Order
item.description: Although the liberal international order was always hierarchical, it was defensible because it allowed for restraint, negotiation, and benefits extending beyond the inner circle. As that internal moderating project fails, all that will remain is the old hierarchy and whatever resentments it engenders.

item.title: The Declaration of Independence and Indigenous Nations
item.description: America’s swaggering foreign policy, as displayed most recently in US President Donald Trump’s unprovoked bombing campaign against Iran, can be traced back to its founding. The Declaration of Independence’s vitriol toward Indigenous people illustrates how conquest underpinned the ideal of liberty.

item.title: America’s Founders vs. Oligarchy
item.description: US President Donald Trump's administration argues that restoring America's former "greatness" requires a rejection of immigration, the aggressive use of tariffs, and no limits on the concentration of wealth. The Declaration of Independence—and the Patriot vision on which it was based—argues precisely the opposite.

item.description: The G7 summit must recognize that extreme disparities in income, wealth, opportunity, and political influence are not only a defining feature of the global economy but also a major factor in many other shared challenges. For too long, the issue has been treated as a symptom rather than a cause.

item.description: The new "comprehensive strategic partnership" between Tbilisi and Beijing tells us more about Georgian Dream's political anxieties than about any genuine deepening of bilateral ties.

item.title: Europeans Are the G7’s Grownups
item.description: Slowly but surely, the European Union is taking steps to reduce its current-account surplus and direct more of its substantial savings into domestic investment and growth. Unfortunately, at the G7 Summit in France this month, the United States will refuse to address global imbalances, including its own.

item.description: While TNI’s past counterterrorism involvement has proved beneficial, it is important not to overlook how a further expansion of its role could complicate existing efforts.

item.title: Modi Is Rigging Indian Democracy
item.description: Under the guise of routine voter-roll maintenance, India’s Election Commission has purged tens of millions of voters, disproportionately affecting opposition strongholds, the poor, and Muslim minorities. The result is an electoral system increasingly designed to favor the ruling Bharatiya Janata Party.

item.title: Learning to Govern a Fragmented World
item.description: As the postwar multilateral order gives way to a multipolar and more contested world, global governance must adapt or risk irrelevance. The upcoming G7 summit offers a unique opportunity to champion a coalition-based approach that translates shared interests into collective action.

item.title: How Russia Could Escalate
item.description: With progress in Ukraine grinding to a halt, Moscow could seek disruption off the battlefield.

item.title: Cognition for Sale
item.description: AI is doing what no previous technology could, turning tacit human knowledge into a product that can be extracted, priced, and distributed at scale. Yet the same technology that democratizes cognitive labor may also weaken the human capacities on which complex societies depend.

Even if a major correction is in the offing, however, it will not happen before SpaceX’s massive initial public offering draws in yet more leveraged investment, nor will it destroy the market power of the other tech giants that have spent years perfecting anti-competitive tactics that largely escape regulators’ purview.

item.title: The Enduring Enigma of Economic Growth
item.description: Developing countries have made enormous progress in many of the factors behind growth, reflected in stronger institutions, rising education levels, higher life expectancy, and increased investment rates. Yet income convergence with advanced economies has been painfully slow—and in most cases absent.

item.title: What Comes After Global Order
item.description: Rather than a new international framework, we need a system for moving from one framework to the next.

item.title: What the Hormuz Energy Crisis is Teaching Taiwan About Marine Insurance
item.description: Taipei’s energy supply is secured for now, but it faces a separate concern of whether insurers will cover the ships that are needed to deliver it.

item.title: Is the US Stock Market Too Big?
item.description: With China and India having grown so fast as to compensate for the underperformance of their fellow BRICS (Brazil, Russia, and South Africa), the center of gravity in the global economy continues to shift eastward. The question now is whether—and when—equity valuations will reflect this underlying reality.

item.title: Exporting U.S. Military AI Won’t Be Easy
item.description: Middle powers have alternatives, and AI labs have opinions.

item.title: Democratizing Technology: India Stack 3.0 and the Future of Digital Public Infrastructure
item.description: Dr. Pramod Varma joins host Tushar Shetty to discuss the design philosophy and the next stages of India's Digital Public Infrastructure.

item.title: Progressives Should Show Real Solidarity With China
item.description: Don’t let Chinamaxxing come at the expense of meaningful engagement with Chinese workers and activists.

item.title: Australia-Germany 2+2 Ministerial Highlights an Expanding Network of Partners
item.description: Canberra likely hopes that its relationship with Berlin will come to hold a similar importance as those with Washington, London, and Tokyo.

item.title: A Death in Detention Puts Tajikistan’s Police on Trial
item.description: Six Dushanbe officers face a rare prosecution over the death of Maksudjon Saidov in custody but none of them is charged with torture.

item.title: Brazil’s Bad Vibes
item.description: A government that abandons structural agendas whenever sentiment turns negative will still be managing sentiment four years later—with nothing to show for it. Brazil’s “vibecession” is a real problem for the current government; but winning the news cycle is not a real solution.

item.title: Ethnic Restaurants and Immigrant Entrepreneurs Facing Crisis Under Japan’s New Visa Rules
item.description: While the government views the new rules as a necessary measure to curb abuse, some observers worry that they may also deter genuine entrepreneurs.

item.description: Software once ate the world. Now it is in danger of eating itself

item.title: Negotiating With the Antichrist
item.description: Apocalyptic thinking is seeping into the political discourse, with figures like Peter Thiel, Pete Hegseth, and Aleksandr Dugin portraying political differences as struggles between good and evil. When rivals become mortal enemies, the instruments of politics and diplomacy cannot work.

item.title: Why Is Europe’s Economy Falling Short?
item.description: For much of the postwar era, European policymakers have prioritized stability and predictability over fostering breakthrough innovation. Today, as the United States and China compete for AI dominance, a stagnant Europe is struggling to regain its place at the technological frontier.

item.title: Why Cambodia and Thailand’s Peace Is So Uneasy
item.description: A step forward in the maritime dispute masks dangers on the land border.

item.description: The Chinese leader’s long-awaited visit to Pyongyang aims to counter Russian influence.

item.description: Within the emerging geopolitical landscape, Mongolia may have opportunities to diversify its economic partners.

item.description: The leaders of America, China and Russia scorn consensual politics. That is a mistake

item.description: “The current situation is not the fruit of our choosing, but of history itself.”

item.title: Big Tech’s Shadow Market Power
item.description: Big Tech firms are amassing market power through strategies that bypass current rules for oversight, investigation, and intervention. Unless and until regulators account for the full scope and scale of these strategies, Silicon Valley’s dominance will remain unchallenged.

item.title: Lebanon Is No Longer About Lebanon
item.description: Iran would rather fight than let Israel decide Lebanon’s fate.

item.title: The AI Revolution Mirrors the Green Transition
item.description: The need for massive upfront investments and the likelihood of significant job displacement imply remarkable parallels between the AI buildout and the green transition. In both cases, the state has an important role to play in guiding market forces on behalf of the public good.

item.title: As Integration in the Greater Bay Area Accelerates, Small Businesses in Macau Suffer
item.description: Drawn by lower prices, a wider variety of products and convenience, Macau’s residents have taken to spending in mainland China.

item.title: The Democratic Dilemma in South Korea’s Local Election Ballot Shortage
item.description: President Lee sold Korean democracy abroad as a system that fixes itself. Now his party and the administration have to prove it by holding the election commission to account without handing the conspiracy theorists a win.

item.title: Where Is China’s Trillion-Dollar Trade Surplus Going?
item.description: Whereas China’s trade surplus used to be absorbed largely by the central bank and channeled into official reserves, it is now being redeployed by private actors in search of yield. This indicates China’s progress on financial deepening and underscores markets' increasingly central role in shaping the economy.

item.title: Xi and Kim Agree to Strengthen ‘Strategic Relationship’ with Socialist Principles
item.description: Touting the achievements Pyongyang has made in recent years, Xi de facto recognized North Korea as a nuclear-armed state.

item.title: How Kazakhstan Uses the Stalling Board of Peace
item.description: Despite opting out of contributing the $1 billion fee required to become a permanent member of Donald Trump's Board of Peace, Kazakhstan has continued to publicly highlight its membership.

item.title: Cockroach Janta Party: Is the Modi Government Wary of a ‘Gen Z Revolt’?
item.description: Hitherto confined to social media, the "cockroaches" emerged on the ground at Delhi’s Jantar Mantar on June 6. What started as a joke has turned serious.

item.title: American Power Is Wrung Out
item.description: The war in Iran has depleted the Pentagon as China’s military buildup matures.

item.title: The Future of Development Finance Is Not Primarily About Money
item.description: Multilateral development banks have not kept pace with changes to the global financial environment. For middle-income countries like India, accessing knowledge and technology is now a bigger challenge than raising capital, which suggests that these institutions must rethink their operating model.

item.description: If you get an important strategic issue wrong, there are still ways to avoid accountability.

item.description: America depends on global order—and can restore it.

item.description: President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. has stated that "the national government is moving and we will not leave Mindanao behind."

item.description: Nigeria’s increasingly close relationship with France marks a departure from six decades of foreign policy aimed at reducing French influence in West Africa. item.description: Israel is rarely mentioned in discussions of Uzbek labor migration. Yet thousands of Uzbek workers have quietly made it a significant destination.

item.description: Affiliation with the Chhatra League, the students’ wing of the Awami League, before October 2024, was not an offense. It cannot be treated as one now.

item.description: A new political center is taking shape in Japan, one that is pushing the old center-left to the margins of the political spectrum.

item.title: Autocracy Is Fueling Mexico’s Fiscal Deterioration
item.description: Mexico’s recent ratings downgrades are merely a symptom of a more serious malady: the ruling Morena party’s efforts to consolidate power permanently. To ensure the legal predictability foreign investors require, President Claudia Sheinbaum would need to confront Morena’s ideological legacy and, more importantly, its cartel ties.

item.title: The U.S. Can’t Exclude China From Latin America
item.description: Rather than pushing Beijing out of the region, Washington should offer countries better strategic alternatives.

item.title: The Trump Economy Is Bad News for Republicans
item.description: Donald Trump triumphed in the 2024 election by continually hammering on the Biden administration’s dismal inflation record. If his party gets shellacked in this year’s midterm elections, it will be because he has made Americans even worse off.

item.title: The ECB Should Not Raise Interest Rates Yet
item.description: Against a backdrop of acute uncertainty, weak growth, subdued wages, and elevated market interest rates, monetary tightening is warranted only when there is convincing evidence that higher inflation is becoming entrenched. No such evidence has yet materialized in Europe.

item.description: The challenge is not just preventing war between China and the U.S. It’s how to prevent their rivalry from becoming the sole organizing principle of international politics.

item.description: The controversial comment was made on the eve of ruling party chief Ravi Lamichhane’s visit to India.

item.title: When Markets Run on Empty
item.description: Markets are flying high despite geopolitical turmoil because a broad-based willingness to keep spending has been matched by an ability to keep doing so. But as more economic participants exhaust their means and rely on debt, a painful moment of truth will arrive—sooner rather than later if the Strait of Hormuz remains closed.

item.title: India’s Eye in the Sky: The Rise of a State-Deep Tech Startup Space Partnership
item.description: India’s deep tech space startups are transforming the country’s space ambitions, as the state increasingly supports private innovation to strengthen India’s strategic and economic position in the global space race.

item.title: Pacific Nations Adopt First Regional Climate Relocation Framework
item.description: Pacific governments have launched the world's first regional framework for planned climate relocation, creating new guidance for communities facing displacement from rising seas, coastal erosion, and intensifying storms.

item.title: Does the United States Have the Weapons for Trade War?
item.description: Economic strategists are pitching tougher policies for an anxious world.

item.title: The Pope and the AI Profiteers
item.description: In his first encyclical, Pope Leo XIV rightly rejects the idea that markets alone can be trusted to shape society’s technological future. Recognizing that AI raises questions that prices and profits cannot answer, the Chicago-born pope has posed a direct challenge to Chicago School economics.

item.title: Pete Hegseth’s Missed Chance to Reassure – and Deter – on Taiwan
item.description: The U.S. defense secretary failed to mention Taiwan once during his closely-watched speech at the Shangri-La Dialogue.

item.title: Could a Democratic Mid-Term Win Upset the China-US Detente?
item.description: If Democrats ride a “blue wave” to reclaim the House in November’s mid-term elections – a distinct possibility – U.S. China policy will become dangerously volatile.

item.title: Why China Weaponized the Thucydides Trap
item.description: It is no surprise that Beijing is now embracing this construct, hoping to shift the responsibility for stability and avoiding escalation onto Washington.

item.title: Bhutan’s Development Model Faces Its Hardest Test
item.description: Today, Bhutan’s development model faces pressures that may overwhelm the deliberate gradualism that has long defined its approach.

item.title: AI Must Not Encroach on Human Dignity
item.description: In a world captivated by technological advances, Pope Leo XIV’s first encyclical seeks to shift attention away from the marvels and terrors of AI and toward the magnificence of humanity. The message that the technology lacks a “moral conscience” and is distinct from human cognition is necessary and welcome.

item.title: Xi Jinping to Visit North Korea Amid Deepening Three-Way Alliance
item.description: For the first time in seven years, Chinese leader Xi Jinping will visit Pyongyang on June 8 – closely following his separate summits with the U.S. and Russian presidents.

item.description: Will openness to foreign players and coaches make a difference at the World Cup?

item.title: Kyrgyzstan Elected to UN Security Council For First Time In Country’s History
item.description: Kyrgyzstan, the second Central Asian country ever to be elected as a non-permanent member of the UNSC, was propelled to victory on the back of a wave of diplomatic support from its neighbors, Turkiye, and Muslim states.

item.title: The Key Forces Now Shaping Markets and Geopolitics
item.description: Three major factors are likely to shape economic and geopolitical outcomes over the next several years: politically unconstrained AI development, a shift from the logic of globalization to zero-sum thinking, and heightened international tail risks. Much could go right, or at least as expected—until it doesn’t.

item.title: Washington Is Testing Singapore’s Patience
item.description: Donald Trump is working hard to alienate yet another strategic partner.

item.title: The Living Ghosts of Violent Radicalism
item.description: Following World War II, the most militant student movements emerged in the former Axis powers as a way to cope with previous generations' complicity. But while the Red Army Faction and others were haunted by memories of past crimes, today's young people have mostly forgotten, and a growing number think far-right politics are cool.

item.title: Using AI to Test Policy Language
item.description: Policymakers have long understood that language affects economic behavior, but until recently, there were few tools capable of systematically analyzing its effects. AI may now help close that gap, enabling officials to anticipate how framing and timing can move markets and shift public opinion.

item.title: Everyone Is Waiting for Trump’s Gaza Plan to Die
item.description: The Board of Peace has been revealed to be so flawed as to be completely unworkable.

item.title: To Fix Haiti, Look to Colombia
item.description: An example—albeit an imperfect one—of how to achieve a lasting peace.
